systems.name: Stationary Head
systems.description: Stationary portion of the head containing end working tool spindles and divided into five positions.

systems.name: Revolving Head
systems.description: Rotating head that holds the work spindles (five) and locating blocks; indexed during the cycle.

systems.name: Cam and Lever Control System
systems.description: Front and right-end cam levers and their cams that actuate cross-working tools and end working tool spindles.

systems.name: Feed System / Feed Gear Box
systems.description: Feed gear box and feed change gears that control machine cycle time; houses the low-speed (roll-away) clutch.

systems.name: Drive System
systems.description: Motor, V-belts and main drive shaft that deliver power to the machine; includes V-belt housing.

systems.name: Work Handling System
systems.description: Components for loading raw bar stock and removing finished parts (wire case carrier, stock paddle, work chute, chip conveyor).

systems.name: Operator Controls (Front)
systems.description: Front operator controls including the hand wheel, three-stage control box and starting clutch lever; locating/locking lever also accessed from front.

systems.name: Rear Auxiliary System
systems.description: Rear-mounted components such as main power box, coolant pump, and Bezier lubricating pump.

systems.name: Clutch and Brake System
systems.description: High-speed clutch, low-speed (roll-away) clutch and brake used to control machine speed during index and work cycles.

systems.name: Spindle Speed and Change Gears
systems.description: Spindle change gears and gear cover at the rear that govern the speed of the work spindles.

components.name: Front Cam Lever
components.description: One of four front cam levers that are spring-return and activated by individual cams to control cross-working tool positions.

components.name: Right-End Cam Lever
components.description: One of five right-end cam levers, each activated by an individual cam with spring return; they activate end working tool spindles in the stationary head.

components.name: Individual Cam
components.description: Individual cams that activate cam levers; have spring return action for the levers.

components.name: Spring Return Mechanism
components.description: Spring return mechanism used on cam levers so they return to rest position after activation.

components.name: Front Slide
components.description: A cross-working tool position controlled by a front cam lever (first position).

components.name: Rear Slide
components.description: A cross-working tool position controlled by a front cam lever (second position).

components.name: Rear Tool Arm
components.description: A cross-working tool position activated by a front cam lever (third position).

components.name: Front Tool Arm
components.description: A cross-working tool position activated by a front cam lever (fifth position).

components.name: End Working Tool Spindle
components.description: Tool spindles located in the stationary head activated by right-end cam levers.

components.name: Work Spindle
components.description: Spindles in the revolving head that hold and turn bar stock during machining.

components.name: Locating and Locking Lever
components.description: Front lever that fits over the five locating blocks to lock the revolving head during machining; activated by locating and locking cam.

components.name: Locating Blocks
components.description: Five blocks, one for each work spindle on the revolving head, engaged by the locating and locking lever.

components.name: Locating and Locking Cam

components.name: Hand Wheel

components.name: Work Chute
components.description: Chute at the front where finished workpieces drop into the workpiece container.

components.name: Workpiece Container

components.name: Burring Spindle Opening/Closing Cam

components.name: Feed Gear Box

components.name: Feed Change Gears

components.name: Low-Speed / Roll-Away Clutch

components.name: Chip Conveyor

components.name: Wire Case Carrier

components.name: Loading Rod

components.name: 7.5 Horsepower Motor

components.name: V-Belts

components.name: Main Drive Shaft

components.name: Brake

symptoms.name: Tools Not Set to Center / Not Cutting Properly
symptoms.description: Tools will not be set to the center line and will not cut properly if the tool setting block from another machine is used (block heights differ).

symptoms.name: Extensive Damage from Setting Block Left In Place
symptoms.description: If the tool setting block is not removed from the cross slide before running, it will cause extensive damage during operation.

symptoms.name: Tapered Workpiece
symptoms.description: Workpiece is smaller on one end than the other, indicating a taper that must be corrected.

symptoms.name: Incorrect Diameter (Too Large or Too Small)
symptoms.description: Workpiece diameter is too large or too small and requires adjustment of tooling position.

symptoms.name: Tool Misalignment (Lateral)
symptoms.description: Tool is not in lateral alignment with other tools and needs lateral adjustment.

symptoms.name: Backlash on Adjusting Worm
symptoms.description: Backlash present on the height adjusting worm requiring taking up slack to maintain center height.

causes.description: Tool setting block made for a different machine was used, causing incorrect block height and improper tool centering.
Tool setting block was not removed from the cross slide before running the machine.
Turnbuckle associated with the front cam lever is not set correctly, resulting in incorrect tool position for desired diameter.
Taper adjusting eccentric and/or T-bolt not set correctly, causing the rear of the tool post to be angled and produce a tapered workpiece.
Lateral adjusting screw not adjusted (or T-bolt not loosened) resulting in tool not aligned laterally with other tools.
Backlash in the circular/height adjusting worm causing the tool height to shift until backlash is taken up.

fixes.description: Only use the tool setting block made for this specific machine; verify serial number on the block matches the machine.
After centering the tool and before running a workpiece, remove the tool setting block from the cross slide to prevent extensive damage.
To make the workpiece smaller: back off the tool post stop screw, turn the associated turnbuckle clockwise (one flat moves ~.014" on hex nut), then reset the tool post stop screw.
To make the workpiece larger: turn the associated turnbuckle counterclockwise, then reset the tool post stop screw (note one flat on stop screw = .010").

Loosen the eccentric locking screw and the T-bolt nut, turn the taper adjusting eccentric in the direction needed to move the tool (swing rear of tool post right or left), then lock down the T-bolt nut and eccentric locking screw.
Loosen the nut on the T-bolt, turn the lateral adjusting screw counterclockwise to move the tool post closer to the revolving head (one flat = 1/64"), then retighten the T-bolt nut.
Loosen the nut on the T-bolt, turn the lateral adjusting screw clockwise to move the tool away from the revolving head, then retighten the T-bolt nut.
After bringing the tool to center by turning the height adjusting worm, tighten the tool clamp bolt, then take up backlash by turning the worm counterclockwise again to eliminate slack.

Clean the tool pocket, insert the tool pin into the hole in the half-moon adjusting plate closest to center, insert the tool clamp under the clamp bolt so it presses against the jack bolt and tool side, snug the tool clamp bolt (just snug) and then finalize centering with the height adjusting worm before tightening.

symptoms.name: Burr/Protrusion on End of Stock
symptoms.description: When the cut-off tool is pushed fully forward there may still be a protrusion or burr on the end of the stock (not a clean cut).

causes.description: Cut-off tool is set too low (not raised to center), causing an incomplete cut and a burr/protrusion on the end of the stock.

fixes.description: Raise the cut-off tool by turning the tool adjusting worm counter-clockwise; repeat until the end of the stock cuts off clean.

components.description: Set screw that locks the size tool and shoe in the cutter shank holder.

components.name: Shoe
components.description: Small clamping piece used with the set screw to lock the size tool shank in place.

components.name: Cutter Shank Holder
components.description: Portion of the holder that accepts the size tool shank and provides a front flat surface for measurement.

components.name: Square Head Locking Screw
components.description: Locking screw on the holder used in conjunction with taper set screws to lock angular adjustments.

components.name: Taper Adjusting Set Screws
components.description: Two set screws on the side of the holder used to move one end of the tool up or down to control taper.

components.name: Square Head Collar Screws
components.description: Collar screws associated with the holder assembly (listed as parts of the holder).

components.name: Fulcrum
components.description: Fulcrum element of the holder assembly referenced in the parts list.

components.name: Fulcrum Plate
components.description: Plate associated with the fulcrum in the holder assembly.

components.name: Size Tool Plate
components.description: Plate component of the holder assembly referenced among parts.

components.name: Roll Pin
components.description: Pin associated with the roll or holder assembly (listed among parts).

components.name: Shank
components.description: Shank of the size tool that is inserted into the cutter shank holder.

components.name: Spring (spring-loaded holder)
components.description: Spring in the E2726 holder that provides the spring-loading allowing the tool to center on the workpiece.

components.name: Tool Clamp Bolt
components.description: Bolt used to clamp the E2726 size tool holder in the machine; loosened to remove holder.

components.name: Clamp
components.description: Clamp that secures the holder in the machine and is removed alongside the clamp bolt during holder removal.

components.name: Depth Micrometer
components.description: Measuring tool used to set or verify the distance from the front flat surface of the cutter shank holder to the cutting edge.

components.name: Vise
components.description: Device used to lightly clamp the holder outside the machine for measurement or tooling operations.

components.name: Burring Spindle Opening and Closing Cam
components.description: Cam referenced as a machine index position for regrinding or replacing the size tool (move to half index 3 o'clock on its slot).

components.name: Front Flat Surface (cutter shank holder)
components.description: Reference surface on the cutter shank holder used for depth micrometer measurements to locate the cutting edge.

relationships.description: The E2726 holder contains and locates the size tool (cutter shank) which is locked by the 7/16-14 set screw and shoe.
The holder assembly contains the roll holder which carries the size tool roll.
The roll holder is connected to the holder body by two adjusting screws with a pivot point equidistant from each screw.
The size tool roll is located opposite the size tool (across from the tool's center line) and together with the tool sets the workpiece diameter.
The 7/16-14 set screw (with shoe) locks the size tool shank into the cutter shank holder.
The taper adjusting set screws change the angular position of the tool by moving one end up or down to control taper.
The holder is spring-loaded; the spring enables the tool to center on workpieces and maintain roll pressure for consistent diameter.
The tool clamp bolt and clamp secure the E2726 holder in the machine and are loosened to remove the holder.
The depth micrometer measures from the front flat surface on the cutter shank holder to the cutting edge to set tool position.
The machine must be moved to the half index 3 o'clock position on the cam slot when regrinding or replacing the size tool.

symptoms.name: Workpiece Diameter Out of Tolerance
symptoms.description: The finished diameter of the workpiece is not within the required tolerance.

symptoms.name: Workpiece Too Small
symptoms.description: The produced workpiece diameter is smaller than the target dimension.

symptoms.name: Workpiece Too Large
symptoms.description: The produced workpiece diameter is larger than the target dimension.

symptoms.name: Taper: Smaller in Front, Larger in Rear
symptoms.description: The front (near stationary head) of the workpiece is smaller in diameter than the rear (near revolving head).

symptoms.name: Taper: Larger in Front, Smaller in Rear
symptoms.description: The front of the workpiece is larger in diameter than the rear.

symptoms.name: Backlash on Holder
symptoms.description: Play or looseness resulting from improper clamping of the size tool holder, risking inconsistent roll pressure.

symptoms.name: Spring Float Not Obtainable
symptoms.description: The spring float (spring-loaded centering action) cannot be achieved or checked.

causes.description: The set distance between the size tool and the size tool roll is incorrect; this distance determines workpiece diameter.
The size tool roll is too close to the cutting edge (reduced distance), causing the workpiece to be undersize.
The size tool roll is too far from the cutting edge (increased distance), causing the workpiece to be oversize.
Failure to lift the front of the size tool holder while tightening the clamping screw can allow backlash and change roll pressure.
Incorrect angular adjustment of the tool (set screws not correctly set) causes taper errors (front vs rear diameter differences).
Incorrect angular adjustment of the tool (set screws not correctly set) causing opposite taper (front larger, rear smaller).

fixes.description: To make the workpiece smaller: loosen the rear adjusting screw on the size tool roll holder, then tighten the front adjusting screw (reduces distance between roll and tool).
To make the workpiece larger: loosen the front adjusting screw on the size tool roll holder, then tighten the back adjusting screw (increases distance between roll and tool).
When tightening the clamping screw during installation, lift up the front of the size tool holder and hold it up while tightening the bolt to eliminate backlash and maintain roll pressure.

If the workpiece is smaller in the front and larger in the rear: loosen the set screw on the bottom of the holder, loosen the square head locking screw slightly, tighten the top set screw, then tighten the locking screw.

If the workpiece is larger in the front and smaller in the rear: loosen the set screw on the top, loosen the square head locking screw slightly, tighten the set screw on the bottom, then tighten the locking screw.
For initial setting, use a part or material of correct size and adjust the roll so the material just passes between the roll and the cutting edge.

To regrind or replace the size tool: move the machine to half index 3 o'clock on the slot on the burring spindle opening and closing cam, loosen the tool clamp bolt, remove the clamp and holder, regrind or replace the tool, reinstall, measure with a depth micrometer from the front flat surface to the cutting edge so the edge is exactly where it was, then tighten the 7/16-14 set screw.
Clean the tool pocket (receiving area) thoroughly before installation to ensure proper seating and clamping of the size tool holder.

systems.name: End Working Tools
systems.description: Tools located in tool holders in the stationary head that perform sequential end operations on the stock.

systems.name: Stationary Head Tool Spindles
systems.description: Tool spindles in the stationary head that contain tool holders for end working tools.

systems.name: Cross Working Tools
systems.description: Cross working tools that operate in coordination with end working tools to machine a workpiece (referenced as previously covered).

systems.name: Cam System
systems.description: Includes the burring spindle and bearing spindle opening/closing cam slots used to index and position spindles and tools.

systems.name: Revolving Head
systems.description: Revolving head assembly referenced as adjacent to the first position tool spindle, used during holder removal.

components.name: E-2717 Adjustable Drill Holder
components.description: Adjustable drill holder designed to correct misalignment between tool spindles and work spindles; holds a drill in a split bushing.

components.name: E2763-10 Combination Holder
components.description: Combination stock stop, adjustable drill, and face tool holder typically used in first position; can serve as stock stop, center drill/drill holder, face tool holder or combinations.

components.name: Split Bushing
components.description: Bushing used in drill holders to receive and locate the drill; clamped by a set screw.

components.name: 5/16 Square Head Set Screw
components.description: Set screw that clamps the split bushing in the drill holder by pressing on the flat of the bushing.

components.name: 5/16-18 Square Head Clamping Screw
components.description: Clamping screw used to clamp the center drill or face tool in the E2763-10 holder.

components.name: 3/8 Square Head Bolt (Clamping Collar)
components.description: 3/8 square head bolt in the tool spindle clamping collar used to clamp the holder in the spindle.

components.name: Stock Stop
components.description: Auxiliary stock stop mounted on the front of the E2763-10 holder to position and control feed length of stock.

components.name: Stock Stop Latch
components.description: Latch on the rear of the first position tool spindle that engages the stock stop; must be engaged to prevent extra stock feed.

components.name: Cam Lever Handle
components.description: Handle used to relieve spring tension on a latch so the spindle can move to the cam low point for holder removal.

components.name: Turnbuckle on Right-End Cam Lever
components.description: Turnbuckle used to adjust drill/face tool position relative to stock length; one flat move = 14 thousandths per hex flat of nut.

components.name: Adjusting Screw in Shank
components.description: Screw in the shank of the holder used to move the drill in or out to set depth or center position.

components.name: Philister Head Screws
components.description: Two screws used to mount the stock stop on the front of the E2763-10 holder.

components.name: Face Tool
components.description: Tool mounted in holder that machines the end of the workpiece to maintain a particular length.

components.name: Center Drill
components.description: Drill mounted in the holder to establish a conical, centrally located indentation (center) in the workpiece.

components.name: Tool Spindle Clamping Collar
components.description: Collar on the tool spindle containing the clamping bolt that secures the tool holder shank.

components.name: Tool Shank Hole in Spindle
components.description: Hole in the spindle where the tool holder shank is inserted; holder must be pushed all the way in.

components.name: Tool Holder Shank
components.description: Shank of the tool holder that inserts into the tool shank hole in the spindle and must bottom out to prevent problems.

components.name: Starting Clutch
components.description: Clutch used to start the machine; can be engaged or disengaged during setup and centering procedures.

components.name: Burring Spindle Opening/Closing Cam
components.description: Cam with slot positions referenced (half index/3 o'clock, 9 o'clock) used for jogging and positioning spindles and holders.

components.name: Bearing Spindle Opening/Closing Cam
components.description: Cam slot on the bearing spindle used for stopping machine at start of rise (6 o'clock) during centering procedures.

components.name: Two 3/8 Square Head Locking Screws
components.description: Two 3/8 square head screws on the side of holders that when loosened allow front/center section to move independently for centering.

components.name: Vise
components.description: Used to clamp a tool holder when removing face tool or drill for service.

components.name: Revolving Head (reference)
components.description: Referenced structure adjacent to the first position tool spindle used to determine clearance for holder removal.

components.name: Workpiece
components.description: Stock material being machined by the end working tools.

relationships.description: End working tools are located in tool holders contained in the stationary head tool spindles.
E-2717 holder contains a split bushing which receives the drill.
The split bushing is clamped in the holder by the 5/16 square head set screw that presses on the flat of the bushing.
E2763-10 holder has an auxiliary stock stop mounted on the front with two Philister head screws.
Stock stop is mounted on the front of the holder with two Philister head screws.
E2763-10 holder contains the center drill used to establish a conical indentation.
E2763-10 holder contains the face tool used to machine the end of the workpiece.
Tool holder shank is inserted into the tool shank hole in the spindle and must be pushed all the way in.
The tool spindle clamping collar contains the 3/8 square head bolt used to clamp the holder.
The stock stop latch engages the stock stop (on E2763-10) to hold it in position; if not engaged, extra length of stock is fed.
Cam lever handle is used to relieve spring tension on a latch so it can be moved out of position for holder removal.
Turnbuckle on the right-end cam lever is used to adjust the length machined by the face tool (workpiece length).
Adjusting screw in the shank moves the drill in or out to set center depth or to correct center position.
Two 3/8 square head locking screws on the side of the holder secure the front/center section; loosening allows re-centering.
Cam positions (half index/3 o'clock, 9 o'clock) are used to jog and position spindles and provide clearance for removal and centering.
Starting clutch state (engaged/disengaged) is manipulated during centering procedures (e.g., start machine with clutch disengaged, later engage).
Holder is clamped in a vise to remove face tool or center drill.
At half index there is enough room between the first position tool spindle and the revolving head to remove the E2763-10 holder.
Workpiece is placed on the center drill to check perpendicularity to cutting edge and to assist in centering procedures.

symptoms.name: Oversized Hole Diameter
symptoms.description: The hole diameter in the workpiece is larger than intended after installing a new drill.

symptoms.name: Drill Off-Center
symptoms.description: The installed drill is not centered relative to the workpiece, producing off-center holes.

symptoms.name: Workpiece Too Short
symptoms.description: Finished workpiece length is shorter than specified.

symptoms.name: Workpiece Too Long
symptoms.description: Finished workpiece length is longer than specified.

symptoms.name: Center Too Shallow
symptoms.description: The center (conical indentation) is not deep enough.

symptoms.name: Center Too Deep
symptoms.description: The center (conical indentation) is deeper than intended.

symptoms.name: Extra Stock Fed Out
symptoms.description: An extra length of stock is fed out because the stock stop latch is not engaged or holder not seated, risking tool damage.

symptoms.name: Drill Depth Incorrect
symptoms.description: Drill depth in the workpiece is either not deep enough or too deep according to checks.

symptoms.name: Tools Broken or Burned Up
symptoms.description: Tools break or burn up, for example when extra stock is fed out and contacts tools.

causes.description: The drill/front section of the holder is not centered (drill off-center) after installation.
The two 3/8 square head locking screws are loose, allowing the front/center section to move independently and the drill to be off-center.
Turnbuckle on right-end cam lever is set incorrectly, causing incorrect machined length (workpiece too short or too long) or drill depth errors.
Turnbuckle on right-end cam lever is set incorrectly (opposite direction), causing the workpiece to be too long.
Adjusting screw in the shank is set such that the drill is too far in or out, causing center too shallow or too deep.
Adjusting screw in the shank is set such that the drill is too deep causing the center too deep symptom.
Stock stop latch not re-engaged or tool holder not bottomed out in spindle, allowing extra length of stock to feed out.
Improper turnbuckle setting causes the drill depth to be not deep enough or too deep.
Extra length of stock fed out contacts tools, causing them to break or burn up.
Drill may be worn or improperly ground such that hole diameter remains too large after centering and may need reground or replacement.

fixes.description: Run a workpiece to drill position, stop machine at start of rise (6 o'clock on bearing cam), loosen the two 3/8 square head screws, jog to 9 o'clock on burring cam, let machine stop with drill engaged (draws drill to center), then tighten the two 3/8 screws and run a workpiece to check hole diameter.
Regrind or replace tools (drill) if worn; measure and reinstall drill to approximately same extension as old drill.
If workpiece is too short, back out the turnbuckle on the appropriate right-end cam lever by turning the turnbuckle counterclockwise (adjusts tool position 14 thousandths per flat).
If workpiece is too long, turn the same turnbuckle clockwise to shorten the machined length.
If center is too shallow or too deep, remove the E2763-10 holder and move the drill in or out using the adjusting screw in the shank of the holder, then reinstall and test.

Reinstall holder pushing it all the way into the spindle, make sure the stock stop is upright, engage the stock stop latch, and clamp the holder securely with the clamping collar screw so the holder has bottomed out to prevent extra stock feed.

Jog machine to half index (3 o'clock on burring cam), disengage stock stop latch, use cam lever to relieve tension so spindle moves to cam low point, then loosen the 3/8 square head bolt in the tool spindle clamping collar to remove the holder.

Clamp drill holder in a vise, loosen the 5/16 square head set screw pressing on the bushing flat, and manually remove the drill; when reinstalling, measure extension and set similarly and tighten the 5/16-18 clamping screw.
After the drill has been drawn to center during the centering routine, tighten the two 3/8 square head locking screws to secure the front section.
Mark the radial position of any tool holder removed from the machine so it can be replaced in the same position.
When drill depth is correct, go to half index, remove the drill holder and reset the stop screw in the holder shank to seat on the bottom of the drill shank.

systems.name: Lubricating System
systems.description: Automatic and manual lubrication components (Bezier pump, flush button, oil fittings, gauges) used to supply oil to bearings and high-pressure parts.

systems.name: Coolant / Cutting Oil System
systems.description: Reservoir, pump and coolant lines that deliver cutting oil to tool cutting edges and collect chips in the chip pan.

systems.name: Brake
systems.description: Brake mechanism that controls machine speed and indexing; newer models include Belleville spring washers to equalize braking pressure.

systems.name: Starting Clutch
systems.description: Clutch used to start and stop the machine indexing; its engagement/disengagement has a characteristic 'snap' and requires correct tension.

systems.name: Cam / Cam Lever System
systems.description: Cam components including opening/closing cams, cam levers, cam rolls and pins used for spindle and chuck actions.

systems.name: Chuck System
systems.description: Spindle chuck, chuck levers and fulcrum pins that clamp the bar stock for machining.

systems.name: Feed / Stocking System
systems.description: Feed fingers, feed levers, stock paddle and related components used to feed bar stock into the machine.

components.name: Bezier Lubricating Pump
components.description: Pump that supplies lubricating oil to high-pressure automatically lubricated parts; has a flush button to surge fresh oil.

components.name: Flush Button
components.description: Button on the Bezier pump that when pushed gives a surge of fresh lubricating oil to the high-pressure parts.

components.name: Oily Alumite Fittings
components.description: Manual oiling points to be oiled with an oil gun at the start of the shift.

components.name: Oil Gun
components.description: Tool used to oil alumite fittings and to fill/oil the pump as part of operator maintenance.

components.name: High and Low Pressure Gauges
components.description: Gauges to check lubrication system pressure levels.

components.name: Cutting Oil Pump
components.description: Pump for the cutting/coolant oil system located at the base of the machine; associated with an inspection opening for level checks.

components.name: Cutting Oil Reservoir
components.description: Reservoir or sump containing cutting oil; its top level should be just below the chip pan level.

components.name: Opening at Base
components.description: Opening in the machine base beneath the coolant oil pump used to visually check the cutting oil level.

components.name: Chip Pan
components.description: Pan that collects chips; cutting oil level should be just below its level so oil can drain from chips when machine is off.

components.name: Coolant Lines
components.description: Flexible lines that must be visually positioned at the cutting edges of the tools to deliver cutting oil.

components.name: Belleville Spring Washers
components.description: Spring washers in newer model brakes used to equalize braking pressure when the brake is activated.

components.name: Spindle Opening/Closing Cam
components.description: Cam with a slot used to observe machine speed consistency and to check brake performance.

components.name: Cam Lever
components.description: Lever actuated with the cam lever handle; can be lifted to inspect cam rolls and pins.

components.name: Cam Lever Handle
components.description: Handle inserted into the cam lever to exert pressure forward and lift the cam lever; also used to manually close the chuck.

components.name: Cam Lever Roll
components.description: Roll that should turn freely, be free of flat spots, and not be loose on its pin; inspected when cams are changed.

components.name: Cam Roll Pin
components.description: Pin that supports the cam lever roll; should show through both sides of the fulcrum when intact.

components.name: Locating and Locking Lever
components.description: Lever with a roll used to lock or locate components; checked for proper tension by tapping and applying torque.

components.name: Locating and Locking Lever Roll
components.description: Roll on the locating and locking lever that should skid when tapped and remain stationary otherwise, indicating correct tension.

components.name: Starting Clutch
components.description: Clutch used to start and disengage the machine; its tension should have a snap and resist engagement/disengagement.

components.name: Hand Wheel
components.description: Manual hand wheel used to position the chuck lever into sight for inspection.

components.name: Chuck
components.description: Workholding chuck opened and closed during stocking; its tension determines how well the bar is gripped.

components.name: Chuck Lever
components.description: Lever acting on the chuck fulcrum; if broken, the piece closest to the revolving head will not move when pressure is applied.

components.name: Chuck Lever Pin
components.description: Fulcrum pin for the chuck lever that should show through both sides when intact; broken pins often fall out.

components.name: Feed Lever
components.description: Lever used with the cam lever handle to move stock during stocking checks; has a feed lever roll throwout mechanism.

components.name: Feed Lever Roll
components.description: Roll associated with the feed lever used during checks; can be thrown out for inspection.

components.name: Feed Finger
components.description: Component that contacts and pushes bar stock; can become loose or require replacement or adjustment if stock slides easily.

components.name: Stock Paddle
components.description: Paddle used to manually push stock to observe how it feeds past the feed finger during stocking.

components.name: Bar Stock
components.description: Work material fed into the machine; its size affects feed finger and chuck tension checks.

components.name: Work Spindle Bearings
components.description: Bearings on the work spindles that receive lubrication only in the fourth position when the machine is indexed.

relationships.description: The flush button is a part of the Bezier lubricating pump.
The Bezier pump supplies lubricating oil to bearings and high-pressure automatically lubricated parts (indexing required for work spindle bearings).
The opening at the base is used to view the cutting oil level in the reservoir beneath the cutting oil pump.
The cutting oil pump is located above the base opening used for level inspection.
The chip pan level relates to the cutting oil top level so that cutting oil can drain from chips into the reservoir when the machine is turned off.
Coolant lines must be positioned at the cutting edges of the tools held by the chuck to deliver cutting oil.
Belleville washers are components within newer model brakes to equalize braking pressure.
Brake performance is observed by watching the slot on the spindle opening/closing cam; slipping causes the slot to appear to jump.
The cam lever handle is placed in the cam lever to lift it and to exert pressure for checking rolls or closing the chuck.
Cam lever contains the cam lever roll which should turn freely and show no flat spots.
The cam lever roll is mounted on a pin; the pin should show through both sides of the fulcrum when intact.
The locating and locking lever contains a roll whose behavior (skid when tapped, stationary otherwise) indicates proper tension.
The cam lever handle is used to manually close the chuck during inspection of chuck levers and pins.
Chuck lever pivots on a fulcrum pin which should project through both sides when intact; a broken pin often falls out.
Feed finger contacts and controls the bar stock feed; loose feed fingers allow stock to slide with little resistance.
The hand wheel is turned manually to position the chuck lever in sight for inspection.
Work spindle bearings are recipients of lubrication supplied by the Bezier pump when the machine is indexed to the proper position.

symptoms.name: Brake Slipping
symptoms.description: Machine does not operate at a consistent rate of speed; the slot on the spindle opening/closing cam appears to jump.

symptoms.name: Loose Feed Fingers
symptoms.description: Bar stock slides easily through the feed finger with little or no resistance during stocking checks.

symptoms.name: Broken Chuck Lever
symptoms.description: When pressure is exerted to close the chuck, the lever piece closest to the revolving head does not move indicating a broken lever.

symptoms.name: Broken Chuck Lever Pin
symptoms.description: The fulcrum pin for the chuck lever is broken or missing; intact pin shows through both sides of the fulcrum; broken pins usually fall out.

symptoms.name: Insufficient Starting Clutch Tension
symptoms.description: Starting clutch engages or disengages without much resistance and lacks the characteristic 'snap'.

symptoms.name: Loose Chuck / Insufficient Chuck Tension
symptoms.description: Chuck lacks proper resistance and snap when closing, indicating loose tension or incorrect bar size.

symptoms.name: Cam Roll Defect
symptoms.description: Cam lever roll may have flat spots, be loose on its pin, or not turn freely; can cause sudden diameter or length changes in parts.

symptoms.name: Locating and Locking Lever Incorrect Tension
symptoms.description: Locating lever roll does not skid when tapped or moves when not tapped, indicating incorrect tension.

symptoms.name: Sudden Diameter or Length Change
symptoms.description: Unexpected change in part diameter or length during machining; recommended to check respective cam roll immediately.

causes.description: Brake slipping due to improper brake adjustment.
Feed finger tension is too low, allowing stock to slide with little resistance.
Bar stock undersized or incorrect for the feed/chuck, causing slipping or loose feed/chuck performance.
Physical breakage of a chuck lever causing the lever piece not to move when actuated.
Fulcrum pin for the chuck lever is broken or missing (often falls out).
Starting clutch tension not correctly adjusted resulting in engagement/disengagement without proper resistance.
Chuck is loose due to incorrect chuck tension or incorrect bar size.
Cam lever roll has flat spots, is loose on its pin, or the pin is broken causing the roll not to turn freely and possible dimensional part errors.
Locating and locking lever tension incorrect so the roll does not skid when tapped or moves when it shouldn't.
Sudden diameter or length change is commonly caused by a defective or broken cam roll associated with that axis.

fixes.description: Make the proper brake adjustment before starting to machine workpieces; ensure the machine is warm and the brake is clean prior to final adjustments.
Check the feed finger tension and adjust it; replace or adjust the feed finger or pad if necessary.
Check bar stock size and correct/replace if undersized to ensure proper feed and chuck engagement.
If a chuck lever is broken, both levers must be replaced and maintenance should be called.
Replace the broken or missing fulcrum pin; verify the pin shows through both sides of the fulcrum.
Adjust the starting clutch so that engagement and disengagement have proper resistance and a 'snap' to them.
Check the bar for correct size; if bar size is correct, adjust chuck tension to obtain proper resistance and snap.
Jog machine to inspect the cam roll and pin; if roll has flat spots, is loose, or pin is broken, repair or replace the roll/pin immediately.
Check the locating and locking lever by holding the roll, applying torque and tapping; adjust tension so the roll skids when tapped and remains stationary otherwise.
If a sudden diameter or length change occurs, check the respective cam roll immediately and repair/replace as required.

At shift start, oil alumite fittings with an oil gun, fill the Bezier pump, start the machine, push the flush button, and index the machine a minimum of five times so oil reaches bearings; check high and low pressure gauges.
Visually check that coolant lines are aimed at the cutting edges of the tools and adjust the lines as necessary; ensure cutting oil is on when making workpieces.
Check cutting oil level by looking through the opening at the base beneath the cutting oil pump; keep top level just below the chip pan so oil can drain from chips.

components.name: Bar End

components.name: Chucking Mechanism

components.name: Chuck

components.name: Chuck and Feed Cam

components.name: Chuck Opening Roll

components.name: Chuck Opening Guide

components.name: Chuck Slide

components.name: Chuck Opening Lever

components.name: Chuck Opening Throw-out
components.description: Throw-out used to disengage the chuck opening roll from the cams to permit manual chuck operation.

components.name: Cam Lever Handle
components.description: Handle inserted into the chuck opening lever to manually operate the chuck.

components.name: Detent
components.description: Retaining device that can be pulled out when preparing for manual chuck operation.

components.name: Chuck Opening Latch
components.description: Latch associated with the chuck opening mechanism (mentioned as part to be familiar with).

components.name: Chuck and Feed Lever
components.description: Lever used in the chuck and feed mechanism (listed among parts to know).

components.name: Cut-off Tool

components.name: Work Chute

components.name: Burring Spindle Opening/Closing Cam
components.description: Cam at bottom right front of machine whose slot indicates when the machine is in the working position.

components.name: Revolving Head
components.description: Rotating head that indexes; begins to index as machine is jogged toward working position.

components.name: Starting Clutch
components.description: Clutch engaged/disengaged to start the machine or allow jogging; must be disengaged for some manual operations.

components.name: Main Coolant Valve
components.description: Valve that is opened to allow coolant flow to the machine when starting.

relationships.description: The chuck opening roll engages the chuck and feed cam; it can be disengaged from that cam to allow manual chuck operation.
The cam lever handle is inserted into the chuck opening lever for manual chuck operation.
The chuck opening guide is engaged over the roll on the chuck slide (the guide goes over/around the roll).
Engaging the starting clutch drives the machine so the revolving head can index and the tools operate.
Inserting a new bar pushes the old bar end out of the chuck.
The old bar end should fall into the work chute to avoid falling into critical machine areas.
The bar enters and is contained by the feed tube as it is pushed into the machine.
The new bar is fed until it extends just beyond the edge of the cut-off tool (positional relationship).
Re-engaging the chuck opening throw-out re-engages the chuck opening roll with the cams; disengaging it allows manual chuck operation.

symptoms.name: Rotating Bar (Hazard)
symptoms.description: If the bar's end is pushed into the feed tube it may start to revolve, risking injury to the operator's hand.

symptoms.name: Bar End Falls Into Critical Area
symptoms.description: An ejected bar end can fall into a critical area of the machine where it can cause damage during operation.

causes.description: Pushing the end of the bar into the feed tube (instead of holding it by the side) can cause the bar to start revolving.
Failing to ensure the ejected bar end falls down the work chute allows it to fall into critical machine areas and potentially cause damage.

fixes.description: Hold the bar by its side when inserting into the carrier; never push the end of the bar into the feed tube.
Insert the new bar so the old bar end is pushed out and falls down the work chute (use the stock paddle and watch the ejected end to prevent it falling into critical areas).

systems.name: Work Spindles
systems.description: The revolving spindles that hold and rotate the stock; consist of an outer and inner spindle and contain the chuck.

systems.name: Revolving Head
systems.description: The assembly that houses the multiple work spindles and indexes them around the machine.

systems.name: Chucking Mechanism
systems.description: The set of parts on the rear of the inner spindle that open and close the chuck by translating cam/slide motion into lateral spindle movement.

systems.name: Cam System
systems.description: Cams (including the chuck and feed cam) that activate the chucking mechanism and other timed machine actions.

components.name: Outer Spindle
components.description: The outer part of the work spindle that moves laterally; contains the chuck in its front and has an angled inside front end that actuates the chuck.

components.name: Inner Spindle
components.description: The inner part of the work spindle that, together with the outer spindle, revolves as one unit.

components.name: Chuck
components.description: Device contained in the front of the outer spindle that opens and closes to grip or release the stock.

components.name: Angled Front End of Outer Spindle
components.description: Angled internal surface at the front of the outer spindle that corresponds to an angle on the chuck; lateral movement causes the chuck to open/close.

components.name: Chuck Slide
components.description: Slide that moves forward/backward and initiates the chucking sequence by pushing the chuck lever sleeve.

components.name: Chuck Lever Sleeve
components.description: Sleeve pushed by the chuck slide; when moved forward it collapses the chuck levers.

components.name: Chuck Levers
components.description: Levers that collapse when the lever sleeve moves and whose pressure ends engage the lever extensions.

components.name: Chuck Lever Pins
components.description: Pins associated with the chuck levers (part of the lever assembly).

components.name: Chuck Lever Fulcrum
components.description: Pivot or support that holds the chuck levers in place while they collapse.

components.name: Chuck Lever Extensions
components.description: Extensions engaged by the pressure ends of the chuck levers; when pushed forward they push the outer spindle forward to close the chuck.

components.name: Chuck Adjusting Nut
components.description: Adjustment nut in the chucking mechanism used to set/adjust the chuck mechanism geometry.

components.name: Chuck and Feed Cam
components.description: Cam(s) that activate the chucking mechanism and control feed/timing; mentioned as the activator of the chucking mechanism.

components.name: Stock (Workpiece)
components.description: The raw material held by the chuck while the tools operate.

relationships.description: The chuck is contained inside the front of the outer spindle.
The inner and outer spindles revolve as one unit (they operate together).
The angled internal front end of the outer spindle corresponds to an angle on the chuck; lateral movement causes the chuck to open or close.
The chuck slide moves forward and pushes the chuck lever sleeve forward.
The chuck lever sleeve collapses the chuck levers when pushed forward.
The chuck levers are held in place by the chuck lever fulcrum.
As the chuck levers collapse, their pressure ends engage and push the chuck lever extensions forward.
The chuck lever extensions are pushed forward and push the outer spindle forward, causing the chuck to close.
The chuck and feed cam activates the chucking mechanism (drives the sequence that moves the chuck lever sleeve and associated parts).
The chuck grips (holds) the stock while the tools operate.

systems.name: Revolving Head / Spindle Carrier
systems.description: The rotating head that contains the work spindles; it turns (indexes) counter-clockwise and locks after each index.

systems.name: Stationary Head
systems.description: The fixed head opposite the revolving head that holds tool spindles and does not rotate.

systems.name: Work Spindle System
systems.description: The set of spindles that hold bars of stock; they revolve while the revolving head indexes.

systems.name: Tool Spindle System
systems.description: Tool spindles mounted in the stationary head which hold cutting tools that machine the stock.

systems.name: Starting and Control System
systems.description: Operator controls including the three-stage control box (start/stop/selector) and the starting clutch lever used to engage cycling.

systems.name: Coolant System
systems.description: Self-contained coolant system with reservoir, pump, manifold and lines that deliver coolant to each tool cutting edge; includes main and individual shut-offs.

systems.name: Lubricating (Bezier) System
systems.description: Two-stage Bezier lubricating system: low-pressure (chrome lines) for continuous lubrication and high-pressure (copper lines) for periodic lubrication; each stage has its own gauge and there are manual oil fittings.

systems.name: Feed / Gearbox System
systems.description: Feed gearbox located at the bottom right (operator view) that governs feeding mechanisms.

systems.name: Chip Removal System
systems.description: Chip conveyor at the right bottom center that carries chips and residue out of the machine.

systems.name: Stock / Wire Case System
systems.description: Wire case carrier and related components that store and feed bar stock (material) for machining.

systems.name: Power System
systems.description: Main electrical/power related components including the motor and main power box.

systems.name: Guards / Safety
systems.description: Side and top oil guards that prevent coolant from splashing on the operator or floor.

components.name: Revolving Head (Spindle Carrier)
components.description: Head that turns and indexes counter-clockwise; contains the work spindles which continue to turn while head locks at index positions.

components.name: Work Spindle
components.description: One of five spindles that hold a bar of stock; spindles revolve counter-clockwise.

components.name: Stationary Head
components.description: Fixed head opposite the revolving head; contains positions for five tool spindles and does not turn or index.

components.name: Tool Spindle
components.description: Spindle mounted in the stationary head that contains a tool used to machine the stock held by the work spindles.

components.name: Tool
components.description: Cutting tool mounted in a tool spindle; has a cutting edge that receives coolant from the coolant system.

components.name: Tool Cutting Edge
components.description: The cutting edge/tip of a tool where coolant is delivered for cutting operations.

components.name: Three-Stage Control Box
components.description: Operator control box with start button, stop button, and a two-position selector switch (run/jog).

components.name: Start Button
components.description: Green start button at the top of the control box used to start the machine; behavior depends on selector switch setting.

components.name: Stop Button
components.description: Red stop button at the bottom of the control box used to stop the machine.

components.name: Selector Switch (Run/Jog)
components.description: Two-position switch (run and jog) that determines whether the machine runs continuously (run) or only while start is depressed (jog).

components.name: Starting Clutch Lever
components.description: Lever at the front (just left of center) used to engage and disengage the starting clutch to start or stop the machine cycle.

components.name: Starting Clutch
components.description: Clutch engaged/disengaged by the starting clutch lever to engage the cycling mechanism.

components.name: Cycling Mechanism
components.description: The machine mechanism that cycles indexing of the revolving head and operation of tools when engaged.

components.name: Coolant Reservoir
components.description: Tank located in the base of the machine that stores coolant used for cutting operations.

components.name: Coolant Pump
components.description: Pump located on the rear left side (operator view) that brings coolant from the reservoir up to the manifold.

components.name: Coolant Manifold
components.description: Manifold that receives coolant from the pump and distributes it through individual lines to each tool cutting edge.

components.name: Coolant Line
components.description: Individual lines from the manifold that deliver coolant to the cutting edge of each tool; each has its own shut-off.

components.name: Coolant Main Shut-Off
components.description: Main shut-off for the coolant system.

components.name: Coolant Line Shut-Off
components.description: Individual shut-off valves for each coolant line.

components.name: Bezier Lubricating System
components.description: Two-stage lubricating system that pumps oil to vital parts of the machine.

components.name: Low Pressure Stage
components.description: Low-pressure stage of the lubricating system with chrome lines that continuously lubricates the main bearings; gauge should read 10-15 pounds while running.

components.name: High Pressure Stage
components.description: High-pressure stage of the lubricating system with copper lines that periodically (every ~20 minutes) lubricates lower RPM parts; gauge shows a reading when engaged and should read a minimum of 50 pounds.

components.name: Low Pressure Gauge
components.description: Gauge for the low-pressure lubricating stage (located on the left as you face the front) indicating 10-15 pounds while running.

components.name: High Pressure Gauge
components.description: Gauge for the high-pressure lubricating stage (to the right of the low-pressure gauge) that shows a pressure reading when the high-pressure system cycles.

components.name: Manual Oil Fitting
components.description: Oil fittings on the machine that must be lubricated manually (locations are pointed out on the machine).

components.name: Feed Gearbox
components.description: Gearbox located at the bottom right (operator view) associated with feeds.

components.name: Chip Conveyor
components.description: Conveyor at the right bottom center that carries chips and residue out of the machine.

components.name: Motor
components.description: Motor located at the bottom left as you face the machine from the front.

components.name: Main Power Box
components.description: Main electrical/power box located at the rear (to the right as you face the rear).

components.name: Wire Case Carrier
components.description: Long container that extends from the left end of the machine and holds the bar stock/material from which parts will be machined.

components.name: Bar Stock
components.description: Raw material (bars) stored in the wire case carrier and held in the work spindles for machining.

components.name: Side and Top Oil Guards
components.description: Guards that prevent coolant from splashing on the operator or the floor; shown on and off the machine.

components.name: Hand Wheel
components.description: Large hand wheel at the bottom right of the machine (operator position) used as an identification feature and for operator functions.

components.name: Main Bearings
components.description: Main bearings of the machine that revolve at higher RPMs and are continuously lubricated by the low-pressure stage.

components.name: Lower RPM Parts
components.description: Parts of the machine that operate at lower RPMs and receive periodic lubrication from the high-pressure stage.

relationships.description: The revolving head (spindle carrier) contains the five work spindles.
Each work spindle holds a bar of stock (material) for machining.
The wire case carrier is the container for the bar stock from which workpieces will be machined.
The stationary head has positions for five tool spindles.
Tool spindles contain the tools which machine the stock.
Each tool has a cutting edge which is the point of contact with the stock and which receives coolant.
The coolant pump brings coolant from the reservoir (reservoir supplies pump).
The pump sends coolant up to the manifold for distribution.
The manifold distributes coolant through individual lines to each tool.
Individual coolant lines deliver coolant to the cutting edge of each tool; each line has its own shut-off.
The coolant system includes a main shut-off valve.
Each individual coolant line from the manifold has its own shut-off.
The three-stage control box contains the start button used to start the machine.
The three-stage control box contains the stop button used to stop the machine.
The three-stage control box contains the two-position selector switch marked run and jog.
The selector switch (run or jog) determines the start button behavior (run: continuous after press; jog: runs only while pressed).
The lever is used to engage and disengage the starting clutch.
The starting clutch, when engaged, engages the cycling mechanism of the machine.
The Bezier lubricating system includes a low-pressure stage using chrome lines for continuous lubrication.
The Bezier lubricating system includes a high-pressure stage using copper lines for periodic lubrication.
The low-pressure stage continuously lubricates the main bearings which revolve at higher RPMs.
The high-pressure stage periodically lubricates parts operating at lower RPMs.
The low-pressure stage has its own gauge indicating oil pressure (should read 10-15 pounds while running).
The high-pressure stage has its own gauge that shows a reading when the system engages periodically (minimum 50 pounds indicated).
The stationary head is located directly opposite the revolving head (spatial relationship; they work together during machining).
The chip conveyor is the component that carries chips or residue out of the machine.

symptoms.description: Chatter marks appear on parts produced from every spindle

symptoms.name: Chatter occurs on all parts and a single tool cannot be identified as the source
symptoms.description: Chatter present on all parts but no single offending tool was isolated

symptoms.name: The finish of the drilled hole is not correct
symptoms.description: Surface finish inside drilled hole is poor or incorrect

symptoms.name: A diameter or feature location is not correct
symptoms.description: A diameter dimension or location/position of a feature is incorrect

symptoms.name: The surface finish problem is chatter
symptoms.description: Poor surface finish caused by chatter

symptoms.name: Surface finish is poor but it is not chatter
symptoms.description: Poor surface finish that is not caused by chatter

symptoms.name: The length of the workpieces is not correct
symptoms.description: Parts are coming out too long or too short

symptoms.name: A drilled feature is incorrect
symptoms.description: A drilled feature (depth, diameter, concentricity) is incorrect

causes.description: Do all parts have the concentricity problem?
Is spot drill chipped, worn, or broken?
Is drill collet cracked or burred?
Is drill holder seat cracked?
Is the drill collet nut secure?
Is the drill holder mis-aligned with the spindle centerline?
Is the drill saddle mis-aligned with the spindle centerline?
Do drills contact during high speed approach portion of cycle?
Are dull cross slide tools pushing stock during cut?
Is positive stop tension correct?
Is the correct type and size of cutoff tool installed?
Does a tip or burr appear on the end of the workpiece?
Is the cutoff tool dull? (TIP branch)
Is the cutoff tool below center? (TIP branch)
Does the cutoff slide advance far enough? (TIP branch)
Is the cutoff tool above center? (BURR branch)
Is the cutoff tool dull? (BURR branch)
Is the size or type of bar stock not correct, or is stock bent?
Can you identify the tool which created the chatter marks?
Is it the correct tool as called for in the setup document?
Is the tool on center?
Is the tool sharp and in good condition?
After sharpening, was the tool properly honed?
Is tool firmly mounted into the tool holder? (Check dove tail.)
Is the tool holder firmly secured to the slide?
Is the chatter being caused by a drill at this position?
Is the gib on the slide properly adjusted?
Is the correct cam installed on the slide?
Are the correct Feed and Speed Gears installed?
Is the machine securely mounted to the floor?
Are bars vibrating in stock reel tubes?
Is there an external source of vibration within the shop or outside the shop?
Is final drill, or are previous drills, correct size and type?
Is final drill dull?
Is coolant reaching the drill point?
Are drill margins rubbing on hole?
Are chips clogging drill flutes?
Are features incorrectly located?
Do parts from different spindles show the same problem (sample to locate bad spindle)?
Does the form tool contact the drill at the high point of the stroke?
Is the tool dull?
Is the depth of cut set correctly?
Is positive stop tension properly set?
Are chips between the positive stop and compensating screw?
Is the cross-slide gib improperly adjusted?
Are there flat spots on the cross-slide cam roller?
Is a cam loose?
Does chatter appear on all parts from all spindles?
Is the tool dull, burned or chipped?
Is the coolant failing to reach the cutting edges of the tools?
Is the tool above or below center?
Is the size correct before the finish tool?
Are the correct feed and speed gears installed?
Is the correct cam installed on the finishing tool’s cross slide?
Are there flat spots on cam rollers?
Are the workpieces too long?
Do all parts come out too long?
Is there a flat spot on the center tool slide cam roller or tool slide cam?
Is the stock-stop adjustment correct?
Is collet grip and tension correct?
Does the collet fully open during the feed stroke?
Is pusher movement correct and is the bar bent, twisted, rusty or dirty?
Is the finish of the drilled hole correct?
Do all workpieces have the same depth problem?
Was drill position or positive stop set correctly?
Is it the correct size drill?
Is the drill dull?
Is the drill bent?
Are the cutting edges of the drill the same length?
Are cross slide tools hitting the drill?
Is drill holder on center?

fixes.description: Replace the spot drill.
Replace the drill collet.
Replace drill holder.
Tighten or replace drill collet nut.
Clean and align drill holder with spindle centerline.
Remove, clean and install drill saddle, or replace it.
Ensure correct tool slide cam is installed, set drill positions properly; notify maintenance if brake/clutch not shifting properly.
Sharpen or replace cross slide tools.
Set positive stop tension.
Replace the cutoff tool with correct type/size.
Sharpen or replace the cutoff tool (TIP).
Set cutoff tool on-center (TIP branch).
Adjust depth of cut of cutoff slide.
Center the cutoff tool (BURR branch).
Sharpen and/or replace the cutoff tool (BURR branch).
Remove all bar stock and install correct size/type; reset collet tension.
Return to page 5 (follow cross-checks for systemic chatter).
Install the correct tool as called for in setup documents.
Center tool using the approved procedure.
Remove, sharpen and/or replace the tool.
Remove the tool and properly hone it after sharpening.
Remove, clean and remount tool; check holder for damage and dovetail fit.
Remove, clean and remount holder to slide.
Rotate the drill 90° or change the drill.
If authorized, adjust gib; otherwise notify maintenance.
Check documentation and install correct cam on the slide.
Change the feed and/or speed gears to correct ones.
Notify Maintenance to secure machine mounting.
Secure stock with correct tube liners and/or install pusher spools.
Check for external shop or outside vibration sources; remove if possible and notify supervisor.
Replace the drills with correct size/type.
Sharpen or replace the drill.
Replace drill with oil‑hole drill and connect to coolant supply.
Realign drill or replace drill collet or bushing.
Replace drill with chip-breaker or oil‑hole drill to prevent clogging.
Reposition the cross-slide tool holder to correct feature location.
Sample parts from each spindle to locate the bad spindle.
Rotate the drill so the form tool enters the flute and does not contact the drill.
Sharpen or replace the dull tool.
Adjust cross-slide depth of cut to proper setting.
Adjust/set the positive stop tension per procedure.
Remove chips between positive stop and compensating screw; adjust screw or notify maintenance if not authorized.
Adjust cross-slide gib if authorized or notify maintenance.
Replace cam roller with flat spots or notify maintenance.
Secure or tighten loose cam.
If chatter on all parts from all spindles, follow page 4 troubleshooting steps.
Remove, resharpen or replace the tool.
Adjust coolant lines so coolant reaches the cutting edges.
Place the tool on center.
Adjust depth of cut of roughing tool to bring size to proper dimension before finishing cut.
Install the correct feed or speed gears.
Install the correct cross slide cam for the finishing tool.
Notify maintenance to inspect/repair flat cam rollers.
Follow checks for parts too long: inspect cams, stock-stop, sample spindles, reset collet tension, check collet/pusher and bar condition.
Sample parts from each spindle to find which spindle produces long parts.
Inspect center tool slide cam roller and tool slide cam for flat spots; replace/repair as needed.
Adjust the stock stop to correct position.
Reset collet tension to proper specification.
Ensure collet opens fully during feed stroke; repair linkage if not.
Replace pushers or feed fingers, and remove/replace bent/rusty/dirty bars.
Reset drill position and set positive stop tension on center tool slide.
Replace the drill with the correct size.
Sharpen or replace the drill.
Replace the bent drill.
Re-sharpen or replace drill if cutting edges are not the same length.
Adjust cross slide tools to avoid hitting the drill.
Check fit of holder and clean any chips from under holder; repair or replace as needed.
